coredata.dc:title: Methylmercury-sensitized “turn on” SERS-active peroxidase-like activity of carbon dots/Au NPs nanozyme for selective detection of ochratoxin A in coffee
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiulan
Han, Qinqin
Yang, Dezhi
Li, Kexiang
Wang, Yijie
Chen, Dan
Yang, Yaling
Li, Hong
coredata.dc:description: The improvement and regulation of catalytic performance of nanozyme have long been pursued with sustained efforts. Herein, gold nanoparticles (S-CDs/AuNPs) with weak peroxidase-like (POD) activity were synthesized by Au-S bond using a sulfur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) as reducing agent and stabilizer. However, methylmercury (MeHg+) could selectively and sensitively regulate the POD-like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs. The catalytic activity of S-CDs/AuNPs was significantly activated with the addition of MeHg+, resulting in a significant enhancement of electromagnetic fields to present an obvious SERS signal. More intriguingly, the introduction of ochratoxin A (OTA) could simultaneously turn off the UV–vis absorbance signals and the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signal. Based on these findings, a selective colorimetric-SERS dual-mode OTA detection strategy was established with gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) as the probe, and the low limit of detection (LOD) of OTA was 0.29 µgL−1 (Colorimetric) and 0.16 µgL−1 (SERS), respectively, with good recoveries from 95.9 to 104.0% (Colorimetric) and from 96.7 to 108.9% (SERS), respectively. The work paves a new way to design nanozyme-based colorimetric and SERS protocol for traces OTA residues analysis in foodstuff analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methylmercury
Ochratoxin A
Peroxidase-like activity
SERS
Nanozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and SERS measurements of OTA
MeHg+-stimulated the POD-Like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs
POD-like reaction-induced SERS activity of S-CDs/AuNPs enhancement based on the stimulated of MeHg+
Stimulated mechanism of MeHg+ for S-CDs/AuNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV−vis spectra of S-CDs/AuNPs in the presence or absence of MeHg+, (b) TEM image of S-CDs/AuNPs, (c and d) TEM image of Au@HgNPs, (e) EDS of Au@HgNPs.
(a) FTIR, (b) XRD and (c) surface XPS spectra of AuNPs/S-CDs and Au@HgNPs, High-resolution XPS spectra of (d) S 2p, (e) Au 4f and (f) Hg 4f of nanozymes.

(a) Absorption spectra of the oxidation reaction of TMB in the presence of S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, (b) UV–vis spectra of S-CDs/AuNPs-H2O2-TMB system with different concentrations of MeHg+, Steady-state kinetic analysis of (c) S-CDs/AuNPs and (d) Au@HgNPs for H2O2 substrate, Steady-state kinetic analysis of (e) S-CDs/AuNPs and (f) Au@HgNPs for TMB substrate. The inset shows the calibration curve.

(a) SERS spectra of the POD-like reaction system of S-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs, (b) the selective activation of MeHg+ for SERS activity of S-CDs/AuNPs, (c) different concentrations of MeHg+-stimulated the POD-like reaction-induced SERS activity of S-CDs/AuNPs enhancement, (d) DFT simulated SERS and SERS spectra of S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs.

OH detection by the (a) degradation of MV and (b) oxidation of PTA; (c) catalytic activity of Au@HgNPs with different ROS scavengers (0.05 mM t-BuOH as
OH scavengers and 2 mM TEMPOL as
O2 – scavengers) in the presence of H2O2 (1.25 mM); (d)
O2 –, (e)⋅OH and (f)
CH3 studied by the ESR spectroscopy; (g) 1D 1H NMR spectra recorded; (h) DLS analysis for size measurement; (i) zeta potential for surface charge measurement; (j) comparison of the time-dependent catalytic effects of nanozymes under the different condition; selectivity of Au@HgNPs/H2O2 + TMB system for OTA detection: (k) UV–vis spectra and (l) SERS spectra; (m) colorimetric linear curve and (n) SERS linear curve of the solution at constant concentration of OTA (Inset: the corresponding spectra changes of the solution systems).
Schematic illustration of MeHg+-sensitized SERS-active POD-like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs and the “turn off” of OTA.

head.ce:title: Methylmercury-sensitized “turn on” SERS-active peroxidase-like activity of carbon dots/Au NPs nanozyme for selective detection of ochratoxin A in coffee

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The improvement and regulation of catalytic performance of nanozyme have long been pursued with sustained efforts. Herein, gold nanoparticles (S-CDs/AuNPs) with weak peroxidase-like (POD) activity were synthesized by Au-S bond using a sulfur doped carbon dots (S-CDs) as reducing agent and stabilizer. However, methylmercury (MeHg+) could selectively and sensitively regulate the POD-like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs. The catalytic activity of S-CDs/AuNPs was significantly activated with the addition of MeHg+, resulting in a significant enhancement of electromagnetic fields to present an obvious SERS signal. More intriguingly, the introduction of ochratoxin A (OTA) could simultaneously turn off the UV–vis absorbance signals and the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signal. Based on these findings, a selective colorimetric-SERS dual-mode OTA detection strategy was established with gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs) as the probe, and the low limit of detection (LOD) of OTA was 0.29 µgL−1 (Colorimetric) and 0.16 µgL−1 (SERS), respectively, with good recoveries from 95.9 to 104.0% (Colorimetric) and from 96.7 to 108.9% (SERS), respectively. The work paves a new way to design nanozyme-based colorimetric and SERS protocol for traces OTA residues analysis in foodstuff analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes play a vital role in catalytic field for mimic-natural enzymes activity due to their excellent catalytic activity. Numerous nanomaterials, including noble metal nanoparticles (NPs), metals and metal oxides, metal-organic frame-works, carbon materials, etc., were found to exhibit various enzyme-like activities (Liu et al., 2021, Hu et al., 2018). For example, MoS2 nanosheets could display a greatly peroxidase-like (POD-like) catalytic activity (Zhang et al., 2022). Sulfur/nitrogen (S/N) co-doped carbon nanozymes could catalyze H2O2 to yield the hydroxyl radical (•OH), and then present superior POD-like activity (Chen et al., 2020). Fe3O4 nanozymes have been found to possess the enzyme-like (POD- and oxidase- (OXD-) like) catalysis activities (Li et al., 2019). In addition, catalase-like activity, and superoxide dismutase-like activity (Lou et al., 2019) had been mimicked by lots of nanozymes. Despite these advances, no studies have yet shown that the regulatory enzyme catalytic activity of nanozyme is comparable to that of natural enzymes. Hence, regulation of nanomaterials with highly catalytic activities is the key to expend the application of nanozymes.

Until now, different metal ions (Gao et al., 2017, Zheng et al., 2016, Guo et al., 2011, Chen & Pan, 2021) such as Hg2+, Fe3+, Pb2+ and Cr3+, were found to enhance intrinsic enzyme-like activities. This situation clearly showed that metal plays a critically important role in the regulation of nanozymes activity. Particularly, the assembling of organometallics was verified to enhance the catalytic activity of nanozymes due to high catalytic site density (Wang et al., 2022). Organometallics, e.g. metalloporphyrin (Huang et al., 2020), hemin (Liu et al., 2021), methylmercury (MeHg+) (Bera et al., 2014, Zhao et al., 2015, Li et al., 2023), and bipyridine ruthenium (Ru(bpy)3 2+) (Carvalho et al., 2018), can improve catalytic activity and enhance electron transfer of nanozymes. Therefore, organometallics provide a new strategy to regulate the catalytic activity of nanozymes.

In recent years, due to some attractive advantages such as low cost, short detection time, and simple operation, the colorimetric sensor has recently aroused increasingly scientific attention. Particularly, the nanozymes catalysis-based colorimetric sensor has been shown an enormous potential on the establish of various targets detection (Li et al., 2020). Although great efforts have been made to improving single-mode sensor, there is still a big gap between the single-mode sensor and the dual-mode sensor strategy in terms of sensing accuracy and anti-interference performance, which becomes a bottleneck for future research on the applications of nanozymes catalysis-based colorimetric sensor.

S-CDs was synthesized by a step microwave digestion (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German). Briefly, 0.07 g of SH-β-CD and 27.6 mg of citric acid were added in 30 mL of DI water, and then 50 μL of ethylenediamine was added to the above solution to form a transparent solution by sonication for 15 min. The reaction mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor, and then heated to 180°C in 1 min in 850 W microwave irradiations for 1 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 7155 g for 15 min and filtered through the 0.22 μm membrane. The S-CDs was stored at 4°C for using in further experiments.

The S-CDs/AuNPs was prepared by adding 45 μL of S-CDs (0.2 mg mL− 1) into the mixture solution including 200 μL HAuCl4 (1%) and 20 mL of DI water. After that, the solution was stirred at temperature of 95°C for 25 min. Finally, the purple S-CDs/AuNPs solution was acquired.

In briefly, 75 μL TMB (5 mM), 25 μL H2O2 (50 mM) and 150 μL S-CDs/AuNPs were mixed with each other. Then, 500 µgL−1 of different volumes (1–125 µL) of MeHg+ solution was added and incubated for 10 min. Finally, Raman spectra was recorded from 200 to 1800 cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and SERS measurements of OTA
ce:section.ce:para: For the assay of OTA, 75 μL of 5 mM TMB, 25 μL of 50 mM H2O2, 20 μL of various concentrations of OTA, 100 μL of 500 µgL−1 MeHg+ and 150 μL S-CDs/AuNP were sequentially pipetted in tube. The mixture was shaken thoroughly and used for colorimetric and SERS detection after incubating for 5 min.

In the generally POD-like reaction, the enzyme activity was assessed overall by colorimetric reactions using TMB as substrate. A reaction mixture, containing 50 μL of H2O2 (50 mM) and 100 μL of S-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs (0.35 mg mL−1), was exposed to various concentrations of TMB (0.625–6.25 mM) or H2O2 (0.0625–0.625 mM). Finally, the total reaction volume was maintained to 4 mL. The changes in the absorbance values of the catalytic systems were monitored at 654 nm. The steady-state kinetic parameters (K m and V max) were calculated by the Lineweaver-Burk plot of the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation:
1 v
=
K m
V
m a x
×
1
S
+
1
V
m a x
where v is the initial velocity, [S] is the concentration of TMB, Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant, and Vmax is the maximum velocity.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 W and Gaussview 5.0 program at the level of Opt + Freq/B3LYP/6–31 g(d). The experimental oxTMB vibration spectra were compared with the theoretical oxTMB vibration spectra.

Fig. 1
a shows the changes of S-CDs/AuNPs absorption spectra with or without the addition of MeHg+. The pure S-CDs/AuNPs shows an absorption maximum at 531 nm and the Au@HgNPs sample shows an absorption maximum at 664 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of S-CDs/AuNPs shows an apparent broadening with a slight red shift, indicating that MeHg+ was successfully modified on the surface of AuNPs (Guo et al., 2011).

The morphologies of the S-CDs/AuNPs are spherical with good dispersion and their size is about 15 nm (Fig. 1 b). As shown in Fig. 1 c, d and Fig. S1 , compared without the addition of MeHg+ of AuNPs, Au@HgNPs becomes particle aggregates fused larger. The HRTEM images of S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs show that the lattices fringe spacing are 0.24 nm and 0.25 nm (inset of Fig. 2
b and d), respectively, which is in good agreement with the crystal plane spacing of Au (111) (Wang et al., 2022). Meanwhile, energy-dispersive X-Ray spectrum (EDX) analysis reveals the presence of Au, Hg, C, N, O and S atoms in the Au@HgNPs sample (Fig. 1 e). The results of TEM and UV–vis absorption spectra confirmed the formation of Au@HgNPs amalgam.

The peaks at 3440 cm−1(OH stretching vibration of the –COOH), 1711 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 (CO stretching bond), 1403 cm−1(COC stretching vibrations) and 1200 cm−1, in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 a) of S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, reveal the presence of OH, CH, C-OH and CC stretching vibration, respectively (Pei et al., 2022). In the XRD patterns of S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (Fig. 2 b), typical peaks appear at 2θ = 38.2°, 44.3°, 64.8°, and 77.5° are assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) (Katta et al., 2022), respectively, which is consistent with the HRTEM and EDX results.

To furtherly validatethe presence of MeHg+ on the surface of S-CDs/AuNPs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out for surface composition of the S-CDs/AuNPs substrate before and after reacted to MeHg+. The surface XPS spectra of S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs are exhibited in Fig. 2 c, the five typical characteristic peaks at 283.1 eV, 400.2 eV, 532.0 eV, 165.0 eV and 85.4 eV are attributed to typical C, N, O, S and Au elements for S-CDs/AuNPs, respectively. For Au@HgNPs, the six characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, 400.5 eV, 532.0 eV, 165.4 eV, 86.7 eV and 100.0 eV are attributed to C, N, O, S, Au and Hg elements, respectively. Under the induction of MeHg+, the binding energy of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, S 2p, Au 4f and Hg 4f changed, indicating the enhancement of oxygen adsorption with MeHg+. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, S 2p, Au 4f and Hg 4f are shown in Fig. S2 a-c and Fig. 2 d-f. The XPS peak of C 1s can be observed at 282.4 and 284.3 eV for S-CDs/AuNPs and at 282.6, 284.4 and 286.6 eV for Au@HgNPs (Fig. S2 a), which belongs to the CC/CC, CN and CS bonds, respectively. The peaks of N 1s at 400.2 eV for S-CDs/AuNPs and 399.1 and 400.9 eV for Au@HgNPs are attributed to pyrrolic N and quaternary N configurations, respectively (Fig. S2 b). The O 1s spectrum fits one peaks at 532.1 eV for S-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (Fig. S2 c), corresponding to the CO (Li et al., 2022). The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2 d) demonstrates that the peaks at 163.5 and 167.7 eV are assigned to the binding energy for the CSC bond (Zuo et al., 2017). The high-resolution Au 4f XPS spectrum is divided into two peaks (Fig. 2 e). The binding energies of S-CDs/AuNPs at 84.2 and 86.6 eV and Au@HgNPs at 83.5 and 87.2 eV correspond to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of metal Au(0), respectively, indicating the complete reduction of the Au precursor (Yang et al., 2022). Furthermore, in the absence of MeHg+, the binding energy shift of Au 4f is 1 eV, and in the presence of MeHg+, its binding energy shift is 0.6 eV. The results showed that the density of electron cloud increases with the increase of electron loss (Ma et al., 2017, Gan et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 2 f, the XPS measurements indicates that after MeHg+ induction, Hg 4f has a high binding energy (100.5 eV and 104.5 eV), which is attributed to the Hg 4f7/2 and Hg 4f 5/2 of the C-Hg bond, respectively (Wang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MeHg+-stimulated the POD-Like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The MeHg+-stimulated POD-like activities were monitored by utilizing TMB, ABTS and OPD as the catalytic reaction substrates. As shown in Fig. 3
a and Fig. S3 a-c, TMB, ABTS and OPD can be oxidized by S-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs in presence of H2O2 to generate the correspondent resultants of oxTMB, oxABTS, and oxOPD with the obvious absorption peaks at 654, 416 nm, and fluorescence emission peak at 564 nm, respectively. However, under optimum experimental condition, the results showed that the catalytic efficiency in the presence of MeHg+ is higher than that without MeHg+. Thus, the POD-like activity of Au@HgNPs was better than that of S-CDs/AuNPs.

In addition, the concentration-dependent catalytic ability of MeHg+-stimulated POD-like activities was investigated. According to Fig. 3b and Fig. S4 , the oxidation of TMB is closely related to MeHg+ concentration. The oxidation rate of TMB could be accelerated with the participation of MeHg+. Based on these results, the catalytic activity change of S-CDs/AuNPs with the change of MeHg+ concentrations were carefully investigated. As shown in Fig. S4 a, the absorbance increased at 654 nm with increasing the concentration of MeHg+ from 0.25 µgL−1 to 15 µgL−1, showing the “signal-on” mode of our sensing system. Moreover, the effect of S-CDs/AuNPs concentration for the catalytic activity of Au@HgNPs was also investigated ( Fig. S5 ), indicating that the best catalyst concentration is 8 µgL−1 for the catalytic reaction of Au@HgNPs. These results further confirmed that the POD-like activity of Au@HgNPs possesses the concentration-dependence of MeHg+.

The further verification of MeHg+-stimulated POD-like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs was carried out via steady-state catalytic kinetics (i.e. Michaelis-Menten kinetics). The enzyme kinetic parameters, including Km and Vmax , are obtained from Lineweaver-Burk plots ( Fig. 3 c and d), and the result is listed in Table S1 for comparison. When TMB and H2O2 were used as substrates, the measured Vmax and Km of Au@HgNPs are 2.3 × 10−9 M s−1 and 0.7 mM, 10.4 × 10−9 M s−1 and 1.2 mM, respectively. Meanwhile, the Vmax of S-CDs/AuNPs for TMB and H2O2 substrates were 1.7 × 10−9 Ms−1 and 7.2 × 10−9 Ms−1, and the corresponding Km were 2.9 mM and 1.9 mM, respectively. As compared to S-CDs/AuNPs, the lower Km value and higher Vmax value of Au@HgNPs demonstrated the higher affinity towards H2O2. These results demonstrated that MeHg+ could effectively promote the decomposition of H2O2 by S-CDs/AuNPs and enhance the catalytic oxidation of TMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD-like reaction-induced SERS activity of S-CDs/AuNPs enhancement based on the stimulated of MeHg+
ce:section.ce:para:
Furthermore, Au@HgNPs also provides an effective strategy for SERS monitoring of the TMB oxidation process. As shown in Fig. 4 a-b and Fig. S6 , the SERS signal of S-CDs/AuNPs + MeHg+ system is stronger than that of other metal ions. This result showed that MeHg+ can stimulate POD-like SERS activities. Furthermore, the relation of MeHg+ concentration and SERS intensity were investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 4
c and Fig. S7 , the intensities of characteristic SERS peak at 1601 cm−1 increases with the increasing amount of MeHg+ in the range of 0.125–62.5 μg L−1. Consequently, the MeHg+ possessed simultaneous the POD-like activities of S-CDs/AuNPs as well as the enhancement property of Raman scattering, which can be integrated with the Raman reporters of TMB for SERS detection.

Theoretical, as shown in Fig. 4 d, two strong typical bands at 546 and 1601 cm−1 can be seen in the SERS spectrum of oxTMB + Au@HgNPs system, which are attributed to the characteristic of the charge transfer complex (CTC) and radical cation of TMB (TMB+) (Guo et al., 2017). These typical bands are ascribing to the CH3 bending mode, inter-ring CC bending mode, ring stretching and CH stretching modes of the ox-TMB molecules, respectively. Therefore, MeHg+ not only provided the S-CDs/AuNPs with much better stability, but also exhibited an interesting SERS enrichment effect. These results demonstrated that the Au@HgNPs-based nanozymes possessed both POD-like and SERS activities property, which could be further explored for OTA colorimetric-SERS dual-mode measurements.

Based on the discovering that the S-CDs/AuNPs could reduce MeHg+ to form Au@HgNPs with high PODs-like activity, Au@HgNPs could highly efficiently catalyze oxidizing TMB into the SERS activity of oxTMB. Thus, a strategy was developed for the activation of MeHg+ to S-CDs/AuNPs. The optimum condition of Au@HgNPs/OTA system were investigated. For the optimum activation conditions of MeHg+, the optimal pH, the concentrations of S-CDs/AuNPs, temperature and the reaction time were 5, 8 μg mL−1, 60°C and 10 min, respectively ( Fig. S8
a-g). Overall consideration for detection of OTA, the optimal pH, the concentrations of S-CDs/AuNPs and temperature were 5, 8 μg mL−1, 30 °C and 5 min respectively.

The stability of S-CDs/AuNPs nanozyme were researched by subjecting the nanozyme to different pH values and temperatures ( Fig. S9
a-d). It is obvious that temperature had a little effect on the activity of the S-CDs/AuNPs nanozyme. However, the catalytic activities of S-CDs/AuNPs are significantly influenced after being stored at pH value, and the optimum pH value was 4. Meanwhile, S-CDs/AuNPs still kept POD-like activity without significant loss stored at room temperature for 9 days ( Fig. S9
e-f). These results indicated that S-CDs/AuNPs showed robust temperature stability and storage stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stimulated mechanism of MeHg+ for S-CDs/AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
To clarify the stimulated mechanism of MeHg+ towards S-CDs/AuNPs, the produce of reactive oxygen radical (ROS) species was investigated by trapping experiments. Generally, the POD-like activity is exerted by the decomposition of H2O2 to further generate
OH to catalyze the reaction. Accordingly, generated
OH was detected through the degradation of methyl violet (MV) at 580 nm absorbance peak (Fig. 5
a). In the stimulation of MeHg+, Au@HgNPs exhibited the lowest absorbance peak intensity, indicating maximum
OH production. A similar result was also found in the case of PTA as
OH probe (Fig. 5 b), which benefited from MeHg+ activation of POD-like activity S-CDs/AuNPs.

Tert-butanol (TBA) and Tempol as trapping agents were used to confirm the formation of
OH and superoxide radical (
O2 –) respectively, during the catalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. 5 c, the absorbance of TMB + H2O2 + Au@HgNPs system has the largest decrease with adding TBA, indicating
OH as the main reactive species. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was used further to detect the generating radicals (
O2 –,
OH and
CH3) during the catalytic process. It was observed that the peak intensities of
OH and
CH3 increased with introduction of MeHg+. The ESR results were consistent with the trapping tests, which demonstrated the enzyme-like activity stimulated by MeHg+ (Fig. 5 d-f).

To further verify the generation of
CH3, the 1H NMR spectrum was tested in the presence the different concentrations of MeHg+. Fig. 5 g shows the 1H NMR spectrum of 0.5, 10, 50 μg L−1 MeHg+ added to 100 μL 60% D2O, 100 μL S-CDs/AuNPs, respectively, which is characterized by a sharp signal of its CH3 group and MeHg+ around 0.093 ppm and 0.875 ppm. Particularly, in presence of the S-CDs/AuNPs, the signal of MeHg+ was significantly reduced after incubation few minutes, disclosing suitable catalytic activity of S-CDs/AuNPs for MeHg+. Noteworthy, in the spectrum of MeHg++S-CDs/AuNPs system, the 1H NMR signal of CH3 group increased with the increase of MeHg+ concentration. Therefore, the S-CDs/AuNPs is beneficial for the reducing reaction of MeHg+ to metallic (Hg0) to form gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs).

A highly SERS signal was observed probably due to the electrostatic interactions between the positive charge MeHg+ and the negative charge the S-CDs/AuNPs. The increase of SERS signal in Au@HgNPs + TMB + H2O2 system can be interpreted by the size and ζ potential of S-CDs/AuNPs and MeHg+ (Fig. 5 h and i). The formation process of Au@HgNPs nanozyme during the oxidation of TMB + H2O2 was studied by observing the variation of Au@HgNPs nanozyme with time (Fig. 5 h). The results showed that the particle size increases with time (0–30 min) after adding H2O2 and MeHg+. This increasing of size was seen with a integrate nanozyme system that contains all two compositions: the size of S-CDs/AuNPs increased while the size of S-CDs/AuNPs did not change.

At the same time, charge variations of S-CDs/AuNPs with/without TMB + H2O2 and MeHg+ wereexplored by ζ potential test with time (Fig. 5 i). An obvious variety of surface charge was seen with S-CDs/AuNPs + MeHg+ and S-CDs/AuNPs + MeHg++TMB. ζ potential of S-CDs/AuNPs + MeHg++TMB + H2O2 was largest in comparison with S-CDs/AuNPs and S-CDs/AuNPs + MeHg++TMB + H2O2 systems, whichis inaccordancewith similar sizes varieties from DLS measurement. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5 j, the value of absorbance at 654 nm increased along with the increasing MeHg+ and reaction time, and remained constant after 280 s. The result could combine with the results of size and ζ potential. These results explicitly showed the charge transfer complex resulted in the generation of Au@HgNPs to form the ‘hot spots’ for SERS signals enhancing.

As depicted in Fig. 5 k-l and Fig. S10
a-b, the effects of eight toxins such as aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), aflatoxin G1 (AFG1), aflatoxin G2 (AFG2), OTA and fumonisin B1 (FB1) were carried out under the optimal experimental conditions (the corresponding structure was shown in Fig. S11 ). Fig. 5 k and Fig. S10
a show that the all of mycotoxins have no apparent inhibition effect on colorimetric catalysis, except for OTA. Interestingly, OTA also similarly showed the remarkable inhibitory response on the MeHg+-stimulated SERS systems (Fig. 5 l and Fig. S10
b). These results suggested the possibility of Au@HgNPs /H2O2/TMB system for the detection of OTA.

OTA could turn off the UV–vis absorbance signals (Fig. 5 m) with significant SERS intensity quenching (Fig. 5 n) of TMB-H2O2 system. The absorbance and SERS intensity decreased linearly by the concentration of OTA ([OTA]) with the linear calibration curves [OTA] = 0.084x + 0.851 (μg L−1) (R2 = 0.994)] in the range of 0.5–10.25 μg L−1 and [OTA] = −47655.16x + 1052526.11 (μg L−1) (R2 = 0.997)] in the range of 0.25–18.75 μg L−1 with LODs of 0.29 μg L−1 and 0.16 μg L−1, respectively.

According to sample pre-treatment section, the proposed colorimetric-SERS dual-mode sensor was used to determine the OTA in the spiked coffee sample. To identify the accuracy of the detection method, the coffee samples were processed according to the pre-treatment method of GB5009.96–2016. As shown in Table S2 and S3 , the recovery rates of SERS mode and colorimetric mode in coffee sample were 96.7%–108.9% and 95.9%–104.0%, and relative standard deviations (RSD) of SERS mode and colorimetric mode were 1.85%–4.88% and 1.30%–4.72%, respectively, which were in good agreement with the concentration determined by HPLC. These results demonstrated that the established method can be applied to the analysis of OTA in coffee samples.

In summary, a S-CDs/AuNPs was successfully synthesized by S-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) as stabilizer agent and the reducing agent via Au-S bonds. It was found that the POD-like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs could significantly and selectively stimulated by MeHg+ and resulted in the colorless TMB into deep blue ox-TMB. The formation of Au-amalgam accelerates the electron transfer effect, and the methyl group can also participate in the catalytic reaction to form
CH, which further catalytically oxidized H2O2 into
OH and
O2 –. Therefore, the POD-like activity of S-CDs/AuNPs was spired and “turn on” by MeHg+. And then, Au@HgNPs further catalyzed the oxidation of TMB into ox-TMB, resulting in a signal-on SERS response. Interestingly, OTA was used to selective inhibit the catalytic system of Au@HgNPs to reduce the production of ox-TMB, rather than quenching the SERS signal of system. Hence, a colorimetric-SERS dual-mode sensor has been successfully designed for determining OTA in coffee samples with good accuracy and reproducibility. Additionally, the proposed method provides a new general strategy for the establishment of colorimetric-SERS sensing.

Qiulan Li: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Roles/Writing-original draft. Qinqin Han: Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Roles/Writing-original draft. Dezhi Yang: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Kexiang Li: Investigation. Yijie Wang: Investigation. Dan Chen: Resources. Yaling Yang: Formal analysis, Writing - review& editing. Hong Li: Formal analysis, Writing - review& editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (grant numbers 202201BE070001-032), Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (grant numbers 2021M20192118096 and 2021T20200097), and Science and Technology Key Programs of China National Tobacco Corporation Yunnan Company (grant numbers 2020530000241037).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137440.
